draft.introduction: Rāmānuja’s Śrī Bhāṣya is the foundational commentary of Viśiṣṭādvaita. Against śaṅkara, he argues that Brahman is personal, the world is real, and devotion is the primary means of liberation.

translation.text: The inquiry into Brahman follows the inquiry into dharma. Brahman is that from which the origin, sustenance, and dissolution of this world proceed. Brahman is the Supreme Person, Nārāyaṇa, possessed of infinite auspicious qualities.

The individual Self is a real part of Brahman, as a spark is part of a fire. The relation of the soul to God is one of inseparable dependence. Liberation is not dissolution of individuality but eternal communion with the Lord.

translator_notes.note: Rāmānuja’s key move is the śarīra-ātma-bhāva (body-soul relationship): the world is God’s body; individual souls are God’s parts. This preserves both divine unity and real distinction—a theological structure remarkably similar to Paul’s “body of Christ” ecclesiology.
